text: I'm Jacques Fontaine, Thoracic Surgeon at Moffitt Cancer Center, and I will be describing our robotic right lower lobectomy technique. This is a patient with a T-N-N-M-O adenocarcinoma of the lung. As can be seen here on CT scan, the patient has a bulky level XI hyaluron lymph node as well as a right lower lobe primary tumor. Preoperative endobronchial ultrasound showed no evidence of mediastinal lymph node involvement in station four and seven. A PET scan showed intense FDG avidity, not only in the primary right lower lobe tumor, but also in the bulky level eleven hyaluron lymph node. No evidence of distant disease.
We offer the patient surgical resection followed by adjuvant therapy.

The surgeon uses an 8mm, 30-degree camera placed in the eighth intercostal space, along with two 12mm ports on either side, to perform a robotic right lower lobectomy. Retraction is achieved with a thoracic tip-up and a sponge ball inserted from the fourth intercostal space anteriorly, as the infrapulmonary ligaments are divided up to the infrapulmonary vein.

The surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy of level nine and level eight parasophageal lymph nodes after dissecting the inferior pulmonary ligament, using a robotic vessel sealer to ensure hemostasis and potentially reduce the risk of postoperative chylothorax.
After completing the level nine lymphadenectomy, the surgeon exposes the pericardium, preparing for the subsequent level seven lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon encircles and divides the posterior aspect of the inferior pulmonary vein using a robotic 30mm gray load tip-up stapler. Following the division of the vein, the procedure progresses to the posterior hilar dissection.

The surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy in the subcarinal space, defined visually as a triangular area with the infrapulmonary vein as the base, the posterior aspect of the bronchus intermedius and the esophagus as the edges, and the carina as the apex. The pericardium forms the floor of the dissection. The surgeon carefully follows these anatomical borders to achieve a complete lymphadenectomy without breaking the lymph nodes, aiming for minimal blood loss and optimal oncologic outcomes.
The surgeon visualizes the triangle formed by the left and right main stem bronchus and continues the posterior hilar dissection by removing the level eleven interlobar lymph node.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to carefully dissect and remove a bulky hyaluron lymph node in one piece. The branch to the posterior segment of the upper lobe is sacrificed to fully excise the enlarged level eleven lymph node.

The surgeon completes the posterior hilar dissection and proceeds to the anterior hilar dissection, removing the level XI interlobar lymph node. The bronchus to the middle lobe and the bronchus to the lower lobe are visible during this process.
The surgeon performs a delicate and complete pilar lymphadenectomy, enhancing visualization of the pulmonary artery and facilitating safer manipulation around it.

The surgeon removes the level XI lymph node, revealing the pulmonary artery. This exposure creates an entry point for a tunnel used in the fissureless dissection technique, avoiding the traditional fissure diving approach.
The surgeon divides the lung parenchyma superficial to the pulmonary artery without entering the fissure, exposing the pulmonary artery. The artery is then divided using a gray load stapler.

The surgeon uses a green load stapler to divide the exposed bronchus to the lower lobe. The dissection of the bulky level eleven lymph node continues, carefully teasing it off from the bronchus to the upper lobe and the pulmonary artery branches. A complete hilar and mediastinal lymphadenectomy is performed.
The surgeon performs a level ten superior mediastinal lymphadenectomy, followed by the dissection of paratracheal lymph nodes, carefully removing lymphatic tissue from the mediastinal region.

The surgeon defines the paratracheal space as a triangle, with visible borders: the trachea posteriorly, the superior vena cava anteriorly, and the pulmonary artery inferiorly. The apex is near the subclavian artery, and the floor is the pericardium. The level X lymph node is removed to clearly expose these anatomical landmarks.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to expedite the dissection process, focusing on defining the borders of the surgical area rather than locating lymph nodes. The vessel sealer provides enhanced hemostasis during the procedure.

The surgeon enters the pericardium, revealing a small area with pericardial fluid that is being suctioned out. The dissection floor is the pericardium, and care is taken to avoid injuring the phrenic nerve near the superior vena cava and the vagus nerve running obliquely along the trachea.

The surgical field shows a well-defined triangular space with no residual lymphatic tissue remaining. The bedside assistant uses a suction device through the accessory port to maintain clear visualization, confirming the completion of the dissection.
